title: Into Your Hands
subtitle: None: the Lord bows His head and gives up His spirit upon the Cross

stillness: At the ninth hour, ponder with a sorrowing and devout mind,
if you have a tender soul.
Be still. The fount of life itself is being dried up.
The sweetness of our hearts is failing.
gospel: And crying out with a loud voice, Jesus said:
Father, into your hands I commend my spirit.
And saying this, He breathed His last.
When Jesus had taken the vinegar, He said: It is finished.
And bowing His head, He gave up His spirit.
behold: Attend to each thing as if you were present.
Behold the Lord Jesus, most loving and most gracious,
His veins now emptied by the pouring-out of His blood, His strength failing.
Drawing near to death, He begins to let His languishing eyes sink,
and His face to grow pale.
See how He begins to incline His head toward His mother,
as if giving her a last farewell —
a farewell, I say, of grief and of all desolation,
which He could not utter with mouth or word for the greatness of His Passion,
and as if commending to her His body, so cruelly pierced, torn, and broken on every side.
showing by His manner of dying that He died out of obedience to the Father, and not unwillingly,
and as if giving Him thanks for calling Him back to Himself —
teaching us likewise to give thanks in adversity,
and as if delivering up to Him His spirit.
consider: Before He gave up the spirit, He sealed His last word, the seventh,
crying with a loud voice and with tears: Father, into your hands I commend my spirit.
By this He willed to declare that from that hour the souls of the saints ascend into the hands of God;
for before, all souls were held below, until He came who preached redemption to the captives.
And so this last word He spoke not because He had need, but to instruct us.
And saying this, with His head bowed, He gave up — He sent forth — His spirit:
not forced and unwilling, but when He willed and willingly,
He breathed out without constraint, sending His spirit out of the body of His own power; no other wrested it from Him.
Augustine asks: Who so falls asleep when he wills, as Jesus died when He willed?
Here Christ gave up the spirit crying out, praying, and weeping.
To send forth the spirit by one's own power is the mark of divine majesty, and belongs to none but God.
For other men, dying, cannot cry out but scarcely breathe; yet He cried, and most plainly showed Himself true God.
So great and strong was that cry that it was heard even into hell, and made heaven and earth tremble.
Mark this: in the same hour the first Adam sinned, the second Adam breathed His last.
In the hour the first Adam, sinning, brought death into this world, the second Adam, dying, destroyed our death.
In the hour that paradise was shut to the one, He opened paradise to the penitent thief.
Among all the pains of the Passion, this pain of death was the bitterest,
for of all terrible things the most terrible is death.
And in Him this severing was most full of pain,
for to that soul and that body the divinity itself was united in the unity of the Person.
prayer: who at the ninth hour of the day, hanging upon the gibbet,
and crying with a loud voice, commended Your spirit into the hands of the Father,
and, with bowed head, sent forth that same spirit,
and, now dead, received in Your side the wound from the soldier's lance:
deign, I beseech You, now and always, to hold my spirit commended to You,
and to pierce my heart through with the sword of charity,
and to imprint upon it the wounds of Your body,
and by these to drive far from it all unlawful thoughts,
and at the last, at the end of my life,
to place my spirit, commended into Your hands, among the blessed spirits. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Quis ita dormit quando voluerit, sicut Jesus mortuus est, quando voluit?
latinJewel.english: Who so falls asleep when he wills, as Jesus died when He willed?
